module_title: Episode 1: Coffee — The Daily Global Connection
title: Knowledge Check: Coffee
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 1: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 12 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here, better than the right answer on its own.

When you finish, take the Project assignment for this episode, where the two big essay questions, coffee's etymology trail and the Ethiopian ceremony as story, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"What are the latitude boundaries of the Coffee Belt?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"15 degrees North to 15 degrees South"},{"id":"q1b","text":"25 degrees North to 30 degrees South"},{"id":"q1c","text":"40 degrees North to 40 degrees South"},{"id":"q1d","text":"Only the tropics, 23.5 North to 23.5 South"}],"correctOptionId":"q1b","explanation":"The Coffee Belt runs from 25 degrees North to 30 degrees South, where 81 countries grow coffee. It is wider than the tropics-only band (23.5), and the 15 and 40 degree options are too narrow and too wide.","citation":"Lesson: The Coffee Belt: Where Coffee Grows and Why"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"Why were English coffeehouses of the 1600s and 1700s nicknamed Penny Universities?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"One penny, the price of a cup, bought you a seat in the day's debate"},{"id":"q2b","text":"They charged one penny for a university degree"},{"id":"q2c","text":"They were paid for by a penny tax on coffee"},{"id":"q2d","text":"They sold coffee for a penny only to students"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"For one penny, anyone could enter, sit, and join the conversation, so the education cost less than any school. No degrees were sold, there was no penny tax behind the name, and the doors were open to anyone, not only students.","citation":"Lesson: The Coffeehouse Revolution"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"What does coffee's inelastic demand tell us about how people buy it?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"Demand drops sharply with any price increase"},{"id":"q3b","text":"Demand barely changes with price, because people treat coffee as a necessity"},{"id":"q3c","text":"Demand depends only on advertising, not price"},{"id":"q3d","text":"Demand is purely seasonal"}],"correctOptionId":"q3b","explanation":"When prices jumped about 70 percent in 2024, consumption barely fell, the textbook sign of inelastic demand. It is not that price has no effect for elastic goods, but coffee buyers cut other spending first. Advertising and season are not the main drivers here.","citation":"Lesson: Price Shocks and Why You Keep Buying"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"The Arabic word qahwa, the root of the word coffee, originally meant what?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"Wine"},{"id":"q4b","text":"Water"},{"id":"q4c","text":"Bean"},{"id":"q4d","text":"Morning"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Qahwa originally meant wine, then was reused for the new drink, traveling through Turkish kahve and Italian caffe into English. It did not start as a word for water, bean, or morning.","citation":"Lesson: The Words Coffee Carries"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"Which country grows the most coffee, about 38 percent of the world's supply?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"Brazil"},{"id":"q5b","text":"Vietnam"},{"id":"q5c","text":"Colombia"},{"id":"q5d","text":"Ethiopia"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"Brazil grows about 38 percent of the world's coffee. Vietnam is second at about 17.7 percent, and Colombia and Ethiopia produce far less by volume even though their mountain Arabica is prized.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and Their Terroir"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"Why did King Charles II try to ban coffeehouses in 1675, and what happened?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"He wanted to promote tea, and the ban held until his death"},{"id":"q6b","text":"He called them places spreading scandalous reports against his government, and the ban was reversed in 11 days after public outcry"},{"id":"q6c","text":"Doctors blamed coffee for the plague, and the ban lasted a year"},{"id":"q6d","text":"They avoided taxes, so he taxed them out of business"}],"correctOptionId":"q6b","explanation":"Coffeehouses had become centers of political debate, so Charles II moved against them, but the ban lasted only 11 days. It was not about tea, plague, or taxes, it was about the coffeehouse as civic infrastructure.","citation":"Lesson: The Coffeehouse Revolution"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"In a typical commodity-coffee supply chain, about what share of the retail price reaches the farmer at the farm gate?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"About 10 percent"},{"id":"q7b","text":"About 30 percent"},{"id":"q7c","text":"About 50 percent"},{"id":"q7d","text":"About 70 percent"}],"correctOptionId":"q7b","explanation":"In the commodity system the farmer captures roughly 30 percent of retail, about a dollar twenty on a four dollar bag. In direct trade that share rises to about 30 to 50 percent, but 50 and 70 percent are not typical of commodity coffee, and 10 percent understates it.","citation":"Lesson: Follow the Money: Bean to Cup"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"The Ethiopian Buna ceremony's three rounds, abol, tona, and baraka, map onto what?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"A three-act narrative: rising action, development, and resolution"},{"id":"q8b","text":"Breakfast, lunch, and dinner"},{"id":"q8c","text":"Three different coffee species"},{"id":"q8d","text":"Three price tiers for coffee"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"Abol is the rising action, tona the development, and baraka the resolution and blessing, so the ceremony is itself a story form. The rounds are not meals, species, or prices.","citation":"Lesson: The Ethiopian Ceremony as Story"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"By 2050, what could happen to much of today's coffee-growing land as the climate warms?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"Up to half of it could become unsuitable for coffee"},{"id":"q9b","text":"All of it will become unsuitable"},{"id":"q9c","text":"The growing area will roughly double"},{"id":"q9d","text":"Nothing measurable will change"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"Research projects up to half of current coffee land could become unsuitable by 2050. That is a major loss, but not the whole crop, and warming shrinks rather than doubles the comfortable zone.","citation":"Lesson: Climate Change Is Redrawing the Map"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"What was the Dutch Cultuurstelsel?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"A forced-cultivation system in Java that made farmers grow export crops, including coffee"},{"id":"q10b","text":"A fair-trade certification founded in Amsterdam"},{"id":"q10c","text":"A famous Dutch coffeehouse"},{"id":"q10d","text":"A variety of Arabica coffee"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"The Cultuurstelsel was a Dutch colonial forced-cultivation system in Java, from 1696 to the 1870s, that required farmers to dedicate land to export crops. It was not a certification, a cafe, or a coffee variety.","citation":"Lesson: Two Truths: Democracy and Colonial Labor"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"How does direct trade differ from the commodity system for the farmer?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"The farmer's share of retail rises to about 30 to 50 percent, often on multi-year terms"},{"id":"q11b","text":"The farmer is paid less than in the commodity system"},{"id":"q11c","text":"It removes the farmer from the chain entirely"},{"id":"q11d","text":"It sets no prices and relies on luck"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"Direct trade pays roughly 3.50 to 6.00 dollars a pound on multi-year relationships, lifting the farmer's share to about 30 to 50 percent. It pays more, not less, keeps the farmer central, and negotiates prices rather than leaving them to chance.","citation":"Lesson: Fair Trade, Direct Trade, and Commodity"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"When you read a coffee advertisement critically, which question about people matters most?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"Who is shown and who is absent, such as the workers and farmers"},{"id":"q12b","text":"Only how attractive the models are"},{"id":"q12c","text":"What font the ad uses"},{"id":"q12d","text":"The exact price on the label"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"Ask who is shown and who is left out. Most ads show leisure, though the heaviest coffee drinkers are adults working 40 or more hours a week, and that gap reveals how a text is shaped by purpose and audience. Looks, fonts, and price are not the core of the analysis.","citation":"Lesson: Reading a Coffee Ad"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"Which conditions does Arabica coffee need to grow well?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"Mild temperatures of 15 to 24 degrees Celsius, steady rain, and altitude of 800 to 2,000 meters"},{"id":"q13b","text":"Hot lowland coasts above 30 degrees Celsius"},{"id":"q13c","text":"Desert heat with heavy irrigation"},{"id":"q13d","text":"Freezing nights below zero"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"Arabica wants mild temperatures, steady rain, and altitude. It suffers above 30 degrees Celsius and a frost can kill it, so hot lowlands, desert heat, and freezing nights are all wrong for it.","citation":"Lesson: The Coffee Belt: Where Coffee Grows and Why"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"What does it mean to say coffee's history asks us to hold two truths at once?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"That Arabica and Robusta are both valuable"},{"id":"q14b","text":"That coffeehouses advanced democratic ideals while the coffee was often produced through colonial exploitation and enslaved labor"},{"id":"q14c","text":"That commodity and specialty markets both exist"},{"id":"q14d","text":"That caffeine has both good and bad health effects"}],"correctOptionId":"q14b","explanation":"Coffeehouses pushed forward public debate in Europe at the same time that plantations relied on forced and enslaved labor. Seeing both is the point. The other options are real contrasts but not the dual inheritance described here.","citation":"Lesson: Two Truths: Democracy and Colonial Labor"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"How does the local multiplier effect compare local cafes with national chains?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"Local cafes recirculate about 68 percent of revenue locally, chains about 43 percent"},{"id":"q15b","text":"Local cafes recirculate about 25 percent, chains about 75 percent"},{"id":"q15c","text":"Both recirculate the same share, location does not matter"},{"id":"q15d","text":"The effect applies only to manufacturing, not coffee"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Locally owned cafes keep about 68 percent of revenue circulating locally, against about 43 percent for chains. The rest leaves through corporate headquarters and national suppliers, so location does matter and the effect is not limited to manufacturing.","citation":"Lesson: Price Shocks and Why You Keep Buying"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"Why is Ethiopia's wild coffee forest genetically important?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"It produces the highest yields in the world"},{"id":"q16b","text":"It is the only place coffee evolved naturally, so its diversity is the breeding stock for climate-resilient coffee"},{"id":"q16c","text":"It is the only region that grows Robusta"},{"id":"q16d","text":"It is the oldest farm in the world"}],"correctOptionId":"q16b","explanation":"Ethiopia is the only place coffee evolved on its own, so its wild highland diversity is irreplaceable for breeding hardier plants. It is prized for diversity, not record yields, and it grows Arabica, not only Robusta.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and Their Terroir"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"What makes Vietnam's coffee story remarkable?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"It went from almost no coffee in the 1970s to the world's second largest producer"},{"id":"q17b","text":"It is the birthplace of coffee"},{"id":"q17c","text":"It grows only Arabica"},{"id":"q17d","text":"It has banned Robusta"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Vietnam rose from near zero in the 1970s to the second largest producer, mostly Robusta in the Central Highlands. Ethiopia is the birthplace, and Vietnam grows mostly Robusta rather than only Arabica.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and Their Terroir"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"Which three geographic factors most directly explain why Colombia grows 100 percent Arabica?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"Coastal lowlands, tropical heat, and equatorial rain"},{"id":"q18b","text":"Andean slopes of 1,200 to 2,000 meters, volcanic soil, and two rainy seasons a year"},{"id":"q18c","text":"Desert microclimates, drip irrigation, and subsidies"},{"id":"q18d","text":"River valleys, monsoon rain, and terraced farming"}],"correctOptionId":"q18b","explanation":"Colombia's high Andean slopes, volcanic soil, and two rainy seasons suit Arabica's narrow needs. Lowland heat, desert irrigation, and monsoon valleys describe conditions Arabica does not want.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and Their Terroir"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"What mainly drove the roughly 70 percent jump in coffee prices in 2024?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"Drought and frost in Brazil"},{"id":"q19b","text":"A new global coffee tax"},{"id":"q19c","text":"A sudden drop in demand"},{"id":"q19d","text":"A worldwide shipping strike"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"Weather drove the surge, mostly drought and frost in Brazil, pushing prices to a 27-year high. Weather accounts for roughly 60 to 70 percent of coffee price volatility, not a tax, a demand collapse, or a strike.","citation":"Lesson: Price Shocks and Why You Keep Buying"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"Which modern institution grew out of a London coffeehouse?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"Lloyd's of London, the insurance market"},{"id":"q20b","text":"The Bank of England"},{"id":"q20c","text":"The British Broadcasting Corporation"},{"id":"q20d","text":"The University of Oxford"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"Lloyd's of London grew from Edward Lloyd's coffeehouse, and the London Stock Exchange grew from Jonathan's Coffee House. The Bank of England, the BBC, and Oxford did not start in coffeehouses.","citation":"Lesson: The Coffeehouse Revolution"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"Which is a real way farmers are adapting coffee farms to a warmer climate?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"Planting at higher elevation, adding shade trees, and switching to hardier cultivars"},{"id":"q21b","text":"Moving farms down to hot coastal plains"},{"id":"q21c","text":"Removing all shade to expose plants to more heat"},{"id":"q21d","text":"Waiting for government policy before doing anything"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"Farmers plant higher where the air is cooler, add shade trees to steady temperature and soil, and switch to heat and drought resistant cultivars, often ahead of policy. Moving to the heat, removing shade, or waiting would make things worse.","citation":"Lesson: Climate Change Is Redrawing the Map"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"At what elevation does Robusta usually grow, compared with Arabica?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"Lower, about 200 to 800 meters"},{"id":"q22b","text":"Higher, above 2,000 meters"},{"id":"q22c","text":"Exactly the same as Arabica"},{"id":"q22d","text":"Only at sea level"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Robusta grows lower, from 200 to 800 meters, and takes more heat and disease. Arabica wants higher ground, 800 to 2,000 meters, so the two species are not interchangeable by elevation.","citation":"Lesson: The Coffee Belt: Where Coffee Grows and Why"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What is unusual about Brazil among the major coffee producers?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"It grows both Arabica and Robusta commercially"},{"id":"q23b","text":"It grows only Robusta"},{"id":"q23c","text":"It grows no Arabica at all"},{"id":"q23d","text":"It is the birthplace of coffee"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Brazil is the only major producer that grows both Arabica and Robusta across its plateau. Ethiopia, not Brazil, is the birthplace of coffee.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and Their Terroir"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"Why does Indonesian coffee vary so much in flavor from island to island?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"Volcanic island geography gives each island different growing conditions, an example of terroir"},{"id":"q24b","text":"Each island grows a completely different species"},{"id":"q24c","text":"Government rules force different flavors"},{"id":"q24d","text":"It does not actually vary"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"Indonesia's separate volcanic islands create different conditions, so the same species can taste sharply different island to island. That is terroir, geography and soil showing up in flavor.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and Their Terroir"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"Where did the first coffeehouses open in the 1550s, earning the name schools of the wise?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"Constantinople, now Istanbul"},{"id":"q25b","text":"London"},{"id":"q25c","text":"Paris"},{"id":"q25d","text":"Rome"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"The first coffeehouses opened in Constantinople in the 1550s and were among the first public spaces where different classes mixed. London's came a century later, in 1652.","citation":"Lesson: The Coffeehouse Revolution"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"Brazil's coffee boom relied on enslaved labor until what happened in 1888?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"Brazil abolished slavery"},{"id":"q26b","text":"Brazil banned coffee growing"},{"id":"q26c","text":"Brazil gained independence"},{"id":"q26d","text":"The global C-Market opened"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"Brazil abolished slavery in 1888, ending enslaved labor in the world's largest coffee producer. It did not ban coffee, and that date is not its independence or the start of the futures market.","citation":"Lesson: Two Truths: Democracy and Colonial Labor"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"What does the International Labour Organization estimate about child labor in coffee?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"About 1.5 million children work in coffee production globally"},{"id":"q27b","text":"No children work in coffee anywhere"},{"id":"q27c","text":"About one hundred children, all in one country"},{"id":"q27d","text":"Child labor in coffee ended in 1888"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"The ILO estimates about 1.5 million children work in coffee production globally, which is why fair trade and direct trade exist as ongoing responses. The other options understate or erase a documented problem.","citation":"Lesson: Two Truths: Democracy and Colonial Labor"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"Coffee is the world's which most traded agricultural commodity?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"The second most traded"},{"id":"q28b","text":"The tenth most traded"},{"id":"q28c","text":"The least traded"},{"id":"q28d","text":"The most traded, ahead of every other crop"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Coffee is the world's second most traded agricultural commodity, which is why its price swings ripple through so many economies. It is not tenth, least, or first.","citation":"Lesson: Follow the Money: Bean to Cup"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"About how many farming families produce the world's coffee?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"About 25 million"},{"id":"q29b","text":"About 25 thousand"},{"id":"q29c","text":"About 2,500"},{"id":"q29d","text":"About 250 million"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"About 25 million farming families grow coffee, roughly 100 million people who depend on the crop. That scale is why farm-gate prices matter so much.","citation":"Lesson: Follow the Money: Bean to Cup"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"What does Fair Trade certification guarantee on price?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"A price floor, about 1.60 dollars per pound, plus a premium"},{"id":"q30b","text":"The single highest price in the whole market"},{"id":"q30c","text":"No price guarantee at all"},{"id":"q30d","text":"A fixed retail shelf price"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"Fair Trade sets a price floor of about 1.60 dollars per pound plus a 0.20 premium, with labor standards. It is a floor, not the market's top price, and it does not fix the retail price.","citation":"Lesson: Fair Trade, Direct Trade, and Commodity"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"What is value-added processing, in coffee terms?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"Turning raw green beans into roasted, packaged, brewed coffee that is worth more"},{"id":"q31b","text":"Adding sugar to the cup"},{"id":"q31c","text":"Raising the farm-gate price by law"},{"id":"q31d","text":"Shipping the beans faster"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"Value-added processing is transforming a raw material so it is worth more, green beans to roasted to brewed. It is not sweetening, a legal price rule, or faster shipping.","citation":"Lesson: Follow the Money: Bean to Cup"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"In Amharic, what does the word buna refer to?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"Both coffee and the Ethiopian coffee ceremony"},{"id":"q32b","text":"Only a kind of cup"},{"id":"q32c","text":"A city in Yemen"},{"id":"q32d","text":"Wine"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Buna means both the drink and the ceremony in Amharic. Qahwa, not buna, is the Arabic word that once meant wine.","citation":"Lesson: The Words Coffee Carries"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"Where does the word mocha come from?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"Al-Mukha, a Yemeni port city"},{"id":"q33b","text":"A chocolate bean"},{"id":"q33c","text":"An Italian city"},{"id":"q33d","text":"A species of coffee plant"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Mocha comes from Al-Mukha, the Yemeni port where coffee first entered international trade. The word marks a trade route, not a bean type or a plant species.","citation":"Lesson: The Words Coffee Carries"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"The words espresso and barista come from which language?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"Italian"},{"id":"q34b","text":"Arabic"},{"id":"q34c","text":"Amharic"},{"id":"q34d","text":"French"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"Espresso and barista are Italian, the last stop on a trail that ran from Arabic qahwa through Turkish kahve. Each word marks where coffee traveled in trade.","citation":"Lesson: The Words Coffee Carries"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"Who are the heaviest coffee drinkers, and how do ads usually portray coffee?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"Adults working 40 or more hours a week, while ads usually show leisure"},{"id":"q35b","text":"Retirees, while ads show hard work"},{"id":"q35c","text":"Children, while ads show school"},{"id":"q35d","text":"Tourists, while ads show travel"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"The heaviest coffee drinkers are adults working 40 or more hours a week, yet most ads show unhurried leisure. That gap between the data and the image is the heart of a media-literacy reading.","citation":"Lesson: Reading a Coffee Ad"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"Which recent event signals climate strain on coffee production?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"Drought and frost in Brazil driving a roughly 70 percent price jump in 2024"},{"id":"q36b","text":"A record global coffee surplus in 2024"},{"id":"q36c","text":"Record low coffee prices in 2024"},{"id":"q36d","text":"Coffee starting to grow at the poles"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"In 2024, Brazil drought and frost, Colombia's long stretch of excessive rain, and Ethiopia's drought all pointed the same way, and prices jumped about 70 percent. That is strain, not surplus or record lows.","citation":"Lesson: Climate Change Is Redrawing the Map"}]}
